title: The United States Will Miss the Old NATO When It’s Gone
gist: Zakaria argues higher European defense spending won't cut the US budget but will make Europe less dependent and less deferential—refusing US pressure on Russia/Iran/China, buying fewer US arms, and forcing German rearmament that revives intra-European rivalry.

insights.claim: Non-American NATO countries collectively raised defense spending from 1.4 percent of combined GDP in 2014 to close to 2.3 percent in 2025.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: Confirms a durable European rearmament trend—structural tailwind for European defense contractors.

insights.claim: The assumption that more European spending lets the US spend less is demonstrably wrong: Trump has proposed a staggering $1.5 trillion defense budget for fiscal 2027.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: US defense spend is set by superpower ambitions, not NATO burden-sharing—bullish for US primes regardless.

insights.claim: Direct spending on defense in Europe made up only about 5 percent of America's total defense spending, per a 2018 IISS calculation.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: Quantifies how little US spending is actually NATO-driven—undercuts the 'Europe free-rides' framing.

insights.claim: As Europe spends more it becomes less dependent on and less deferential to Washington, and may refuse US requests—holding much of Russia's frozen assets, its own sanctions, and hosting SWIFT.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: A more autonomous Europe could diverge on Russia/Iran/China policy—fragmenting Western economic coordination.

insights.claim: European officials now openly seek to reduce dependence on US hardware, fearing Washington could withhold software upgrades or spare parts; Denmark's Greenland worries make it think thrice before buying US weapons.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: A structural threat to US arms exports—watch European procurement shifting toward domestic/EU suppliers.

insights.claim: Losing European arms buyers is a major loss for US defense companies and a larger loss for US strategy, since weapons sales create habits of cooperation and shared strategy.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: Frames US defense exports as strategic glue, not just revenue—its erosion weakens alliance cohesion.

insights.claim: Germany has the greatest fiscal capacity to spend and is now doing so on a remarkable scale, which will inevitably create anxiety in France, Poland, and smaller neighbors.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: German rearmament could revive intra-European balancing—a long-dormant geopolitical risk returning.

insights.claim: The post-1945 US umbrella removed the need for European states to act like traditional great powers; unwinding it sets off a chain reaction that Americans will come to miss.
insights.speaker: Fareed Zakaria
insights.so_what: Argues the stable US-led order is being dismantled—raising long-run European fragmentation risk.
